judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are strong and largely equivalent, but Report 1 fully satisfies expectation (b) by recommending fail-closed on Redis errors while Report 2 defends fail-open, so Report 1 answers the task slightly better overall.

report_1.evidence: call_with_fallback (router.py:13-18) catches all six identically... classify the six into a non-retryable denylist and re-raise those; only fail over on the rest

report_2.evidence: handles **all six identically**, via a single blanket `except Exception as e:`... narrow the `except` to the known transient set and give the other three distinct handling

report_1.evidence: invert the default so the function fails closed unless an explicit, narrowly-named local-dev override is set, and fail closed (with a loud log) on `RedisError`

the free-text content should never appear verbatim (hash and/or length only, if it must be referenced), and the metadata should pass through an explicit allowlist of known-safe keys rather than being dumped wholesale

report_2.evidence: don't log document content at all... "metadata_keys": sorted((record.metadata or {}).keys()),  # allowlist, not wholesale

report_1.evidence: defaults to the empty string with no exception... silently computes an **unsalted** `sha256(applicant_id)`... fail hard instead of falling back: ... raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT is not configured; refusing to pseudonymize without a salt")

report_2.evidence: It silently computes `sha256(applicant_id)` — an **unsalted** hash... if not salt: raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT must be set to a non-empty value")
